bibliographic_data.title: The Ant Swarm Neuro-Evolution Procedure for Optimizing Recurrent Networks
bibliographic_data.abstract: Hand-crafting effective and efficient structures for recurrent neural networks (RNNs) is a difficult, expensive, and time-consuming process. To address this challenge, we propose a novel neuro-evolution algorithm based on ant colony optimization (ACO), called ant swarm neuro-evolution (ASNE), for directly optimizing RNN topologies. The procedure selects from multiple modern recurrent cell types such as ∆-RNN, GRU, LSTM, MGU and UGRNN cells, as well as recurrent connections which may span multiple layers and/or steps of time. In order to introduce an inductive bias that encourages the formation of sparser synaptic connectivity patterns, we investigate several variations of the core algorithm. We do so primarily by formulating different functions that drive the underlying pheromone simulation process (which mimic L1 and L2 regularization in standard machine learning) as well as by introducing ant agents with specialized roles (inspired by how real ant colonies operate), i.e., explorer ants that construct the initial feed forward structure and social ants which select nodes from the feed forward connections to subsequently craft recurrent memory structures. We also incorporate a Lamarckian strategy for weight initialization which reduces the number of backpropagation epochs required to locally train candidate RNNs, speeding up the neuro-evolution process. Our results demonstrate that the sparser RNNs evolved by ASNE significantly outperform traditional one and two layer architectures consisting of modern memory cells, as well as the well-known NEAT algorithm. Furthermore, we improve upon prior state-of-the-art results on the time series dataset utilized in our experiments.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes a novel neuro-evolution algorithm called Ant Swarm Neuro-Evolution (ASNE) for directly optimizing RNN topologies, which significantly outperforms traditional one and two layer architectures consisting of modern memory cells.
core_content.key_methodology: ASNE uses ant colony optimization to evolve RNN topologies by introducing explorer ants that construct the initial feed forward structure and social ants that select nodes from the feed forward connections to craft recurrent memory structures. It also incorporates a Lamarckian strategy for weight initialization and different functions for pheromone simulation to encourage sparser synaptic connectivity patterns.
outcomes: The evolved RNNs by ASNE significantly outperform traditional one and two layer architectures consisting of modern memory cells.
ASNE improves upon prior state-of-the-art results on the time series dataset utilized in experiments.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Artificial neural networks (ANNs) have become a popular tool for solving data-driven problems. However, designing performant ANNs becomes expensive and incredibly difficult due to their increasing complexity, especially when dealing with recurrent connections required for processing temporal data like time series. This study addresses the challenge of automated RNN design by developing an algorithm called Ant Swarm Neuro-Evolution (ASNE), which automatically constructs and optimizes the topology of RNNs, focusing on time series data prediction. ASNE uses concepts from artificial evolution and ant colony optimization to dynamically modify pheromone traces deposited by ant agents, introduce regularization into the optimization process, and utilize different specialized ant agents for modularizing the synaptic connectivity construction process. The results indicate that ASNE can build well-performing RNN structures with connections spanning both structure and time using simple and complex memory cells, significantly outperforming NEAT and EXAMM.
